title: Difficult Airway Management MCQ – Chapter 5
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What is the definition of a difficult airway?

options.B: A clinical situation where a trained anesthesiologist experiences difficulty with face mask ventilation, intubation, or both
options.D: Any airway in a pediatric patient

questions.explanation: A difficult airway is defined as a clinical situation in which a conventionally trained anesthesiologist experiences difficulty with face mask ventilation, tracheal intubation, or both.

questions.question: Which of the following is a congenital cause of difficult intubation?
questions.explanation: Pierre Robin syndrome (micrognathia, glossoptosis, cleft palate) is a congenital cause of difficult intubation.

questions.question: Treacher Collins syndrome causes difficult intubation due to:

options.B: Mandibulofacial dysostosis with micrognathia and malar hypoplasia

questions.explanation: Treacher Collins syndrome involves mandibulofacial dysostosis with micrognathia, making laryngoscopy and intubation difficult.

questions.question: Which acquired condition can cause difficult airway due to limited mouth opening?
questions.explanation: TMJ ankylosis causes severe restriction of mouth opening, making conventional laryngoscopy impossible.

questions.question: Acromegaly causes difficult airway due to:
questions.explanation: Acromegaly leads to macroglossia, prognathism, and subglottic narrowing, all contributing to difficult airway management.

questions.question: Which of the following infections can cause acute difficult airway?
questions.explanation: Ludwig's angina causes submandibular space infection with floor of mouth swelling, leading to airway compromise.

questions.question: Difficult intubation is defined as requiring more than how many attempts at laryngoscopy?
questions.explanation: Difficult intubation is defined as requiring more than 3 attempts at laryngoscopy or attempts lasting longer than 10 minutes.

questions.question: The Mallampati classification is performed with the patient in which position?

options.B: Sitting upright with mouth fully open and tongue protruded without phonation

questions.explanation: Mallampati assessment is done with patient sitting, mouth wide open, tongue maximally protruded, without phonation.

questions.question: In Mallampati Class III, which structures are visible?

options.A: Soft palate, fauces, uvula, and pillars
options.C: Soft palate and base of uvula only

questions.explanation: Mallampati Class III shows only the soft palate and base of uvula, suggesting potentially difficult intubation.

questions.question: What is the normal thyromental distance in adults?
questions.explanation: Normal thyromental distance is greater than 6.5 cm (3 finger breadths). Less than 6 cm suggests difficult intubation.

questions.question: The sternomental distance predicting difficult intubation is less than:
questions.explanation: A sternomental distance less than 12.5 cm with the head fully extended and mouth closed is predictive of difficult intubation.

questions.question: Wilson's risk score for difficult intubation includes all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Wilson's risk score includes weight, head/neck movement, jaw movement, receding mandible, and buck teeth. Serum albumin is not part of it.

questions.question: The Cormack-Lehane grading system classifies the view at:
questions.explanation: Cormack-Lehane grading classifies the laryngoscopic view obtained during direct laryngoscopy.

questions.question: Cormack-Lehane Grade I indicates visualization of:
questions.explanation: Grade I shows the entire glottic opening, indicating easy intubation.

questions.question: Cormack-Lehane Grade III indicates visualization of:
questions.explanation: Grade III shows only the epiglottis with no part of the glottis visible, indicating difficult intubation.

questions.question: Cormack-Lehane Grade IV indicates:
questions.explanation: Grade IV means neither the glottis nor the epiglottis can be visualized – the most difficult scenario.

questions.question: An inter-incisor distance (mouth opening) of less than how many centimeters suggests difficult intubation?
questions.explanation: A mouth opening less than 3 cm (2 finger breadths) suggests difficulty with laryngoscopy and intubation.

questions.question: The 'sniffing position' for intubation involves:

options.B: Flexion of the lower cervical spine and extension at the atlanto-occipital joint

questions.explanation: The sniffing position involves flexion of the lower cervical spine (pillow under head) and extension at the atlanto-occipital joint to align the oral, pharyngeal, and laryngeal axes.

questions.question: The BURP maneuver stands for:

options.C: Backward, Upward, Rightward Pressure on the larynx

questions.explanation: BURP stands for Backward, Upward, and Rightward Pressure on the thyroid cartilage to improve laryngoscopic view.

questions.question: Which is the gold standard technique for managing anticipated difficult airway?
questions.explanation: Awake fiber-optic intubation is considered the gold standard for managing an anticipated difficult airway.

questions.question: The ASA Difficult Airway Algorithm first step in an anticipated difficult airway is:
questions.explanation: The ASA algorithm recommends awake intubation as the primary approach when a difficult airway is anticipated.

questions.question: Which supraglottic airway device is used as a rescue device in the 'cannot intubate, cannot ventilate' scenario?
questions.explanation: The LMA is a key rescue device in failed intubation scenarios and is part of the difficult airway algorithm.

questions.question: In a 'cannot intubate, cannot ventilate' (CICV) emergency, the immediate surgical airway of choice is:
questions.explanation: Emergency cricothyrotomy through the cricothyroid membrane is the life-saving procedure in CICV situations.

questions.question: The cricothyroid membrane is located between:

options.B: Cricoid cartilage and first tracheal ring

questions.explanation: The cricothyroid membrane lies between the inferior border of the thyroid cartilage and the superior border of the cricoid cartilage.

questions.question: Which bougie is used as an adjunct during difficult intubation?
questions.explanation: The gum elastic bougie (Eschmann stylet/introducer) is a commonly used adjunct to facilitate tracheal intubation when the glottic view is poor.

questions.question: Obesity contributes to difficult airway management due to all EXCEPT:

options.B: Excessive soft tissue in the airway

questions.explanation: Obesity does not increase thyromental distance. It causes excessive soft tissue, reduced FRC, and difficult positioning.

questions.question: Which congenital syndrome is associated with macroglossia causing difficult airway?
questions.explanation: Down syndrome is associated with macroglossia, short neck, and atlantoaxial instability, all contributing to difficult airway.

questions.question: Ankylosing spondylitis causes difficult intubation primarily due to:

options.B: Fixed flexion deformity of the cervical spine with reduced neck mobility

questions.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis causes fusion of cervical vertebrae leading to a fixed, flexed cervical spine with severely limited extension.

questions.question: The 'upper lip bite test' assesses:
questions.explanation: The upper lip bite test assesses the ability to protrude the mandible (jaw protrusion), which correlates with ease of laryngoscopy.

questions.question: A short, thick neck is associated with difficult intubation because it:

options.B: Limits neck extension and makes alignment of airway axes difficult

questions.explanation: A short, thick neck limits neck extension and makes alignment of the oral, pharyngeal, and laryngeal axes difficult.

questions.question: Which type of laryngoscope blade is preferred for difficult intubation?
questions.explanation: The McCoy blade has a hinged tip that can be levered to lift the epiglottis, improving the view in difficult laryngoscopy.

questions.question: Retrograde intubation involves passing a guide wire through the:

options.B: Cricothyroid membrane upward and out through the mouth

questions.explanation: In retrograde intubation, a wire is passed through the cricothyroid membrane in a cephalad direction, emerging from the mouth, then used as a guide for the ETT.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a predictor of difficult mask ventilation?
questions.explanation: Young age is not a predictor of difficult mask ventilation. Beard, obesity, edentulous status, and snoring are predictors.

questions.question: The mnemonic LEMON for airway assessment stands for:

options.A: Look, Evaluate, Mallampati, Obstruction, Neck mobility

questions.explanation: LEMON stands for Look externally, Evaluate 3-3-2 rule, Mallampati, Obstruction, and Neck mobility.

questions.question: The 3-3-2 rule evaluates:

options.B: Mouth opening (3 fingers), hyoid-mental distance (3 fingers), and thyroid-to-floor of mouth (2 fingers)

questions.explanation: The 3-3-2 rule assesses inter-incisor distance (3 fingers), hyoid-mental distance (3 fingers), and thyroid notch-to-floor of mouth (2 fingers).

questions.question: Video laryngoscopy is useful in difficult airway because it:

options.A: Eliminates the need for neck extension
options.B: Provides an indirect view of the glottis without alignment of airway axes
options.C: Can only be used in awake patients
options.D: Replaces the need for any backup plan

questions.explanation: Video laryngoscopy provides an indirect view of the glottis on a screen without requiring alignment of the oral, pharyngeal, and laryngeal axes.

questions.question: For awake fiber-optic intubation, topical anesthesia of the airway is achieved using:
questions.explanation: Topical lidocaine 4% via spray or nebulization, supplemented by nerve blocks (superior laryngeal, transtracheal), provides airway anesthesia for awake FOI.

questions.question: The superior laryngeal nerve block anesthetizes the airway:

options.B: From the epiglottis to the vocal cords (supraglottic area)

questions.explanation: The internal branch of the superior laryngeal nerve supplies sensation from the epiglottis to and including the vocal cords.

questions.question: Transtracheal injection (recurrent laryngeal nerve block) anesthetizes the area:
questions.explanation: Transtracheal injection through the cricothyroid membrane anesthetizes the trachea below the vocal cords (recurrent laryngeal nerve territory).

questions.question: The intubating LMA (Fastrach) is designed to:

options.B: Facilitate blind or guided tracheal intubation through the LMA

questions.explanation: The intubating LMA (ILMA/Fastrach) is specifically designed to allow passage of an endotracheal tube through the device.

questions.question: The maximum number of intubation attempts recommended before declaring failed intubation is:
questions.explanation: After 3 failed attempts at intubation by an experienced laryngoscopist, the situation should be declared as failed intubation and the algorithm followed.

questions.question: Which of the following tumors can cause difficult airway?
questions.explanation: Laryngeal tumors can distort airway anatomy and obstruct the glottic opening, making intubation difficult.

questions.question: Post-radiation therapy to the neck can cause difficult intubation due to:

options.B: Tissue fibrosis, edema, and reduced neck mobility

questions.explanation: Radiation to the neck causes tissue fibrosis, reduced mobility, and edema leading to difficult airway management.

questions.question: The difficult airway cart should contain all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Various sizes of ETT and LMA

questions.explanation: A difficult airway cart contains various airway devices (ETTs, LMAs, bougies, fiber-optic scope, cricothyrotomy set) but not a bypass machine.

questions.question: Preoxygenation before intubation attempts is important because it:

options.B: Provides an oxygen reserve (extends safe apnea time)

questions.explanation: Preoxygenation with 100% O2 denitrogenates the FRC, providing an oxygen reserve that extends safe apnea time during intubation attempts.

questions.question: In a patient with cervical spine injury, the preferred method of intubation is:

options.A: Conventional direct laryngoscopy with full neck extension
options.B: Manual in-line stabilization with fiber-optic or video laryngoscopy

questions.explanation: In cervical spine injury, intubation is performed with manual in-line stabilization (MILS) using fiber-optic or video laryngoscopy to avoid neck movement.

questions.question: Jet ventilation through a cricothyrotomy cannula uses oxygen at a pressure of:
questions.explanation: Transtracheal jet ventilation uses a high-pressure oxygen source (typically 50 psi) through a narrow cannula in the cricothyroid membrane.

questions.question: Which of the following is a sign of successful tracheal intubation?

options.B: Presence of end-tidal CO2 on capnography

questions.explanation: Capnography (detection of end-tidal CO2) is the gold standard for confirming correct tracheal tube placement.

questions.question: The lightwand (lighted stylet) technique works on the principle of:

options.B: Transillumination of the anterior neck when the tip is in the trachea

questions.explanation: The lightwand uses transillumination – a bright glow seen in the midline of the anterior neck confirms the stylet tip is in the trachea.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important step after a failed intubation attempt?

options.A: Immediately attempt again with the same technique
options.B: Ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation before the next attempt

questions.explanation: After a failed attempt, the priority is to ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation (bag-mask or LMA) before considering the next attempt or alternative plan.
